expectations.text: The agent fetches inline review comments via the REST API (gh api repos/.../pulls/.../comments)
expectations.evidence: Step 2: `gh api repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/42/comments --paginate` with full `--jq` projection. Returns all 5 comments including the suggestion reply.

expectations.text: The agent fetches thread resolution state via GraphQL to filter out already-resolved threads
expectations.evidence: Step 3: Full GraphQL query using `reviewThreads(first: 50)` with `isResolved`, `isOutdated`, and `comments { nodes { databaseId } }` fields. Returns four threads mapped to REST comment IDs.

expectations.text: Already-resolved threads are not included in the plan or acted upon
expectations.evidence: No threads were pre-resolved in this scenario, so the assertion is satisfied vacuously. Additionally, thread 1130 (isOutdated: true) was correctly excluded from action and marked `skip` in the plan table. The agent explicitly notes 'No threads are already resolved.' and handles the outdated state correctly.

expectations.text: The agent presents a plan categorizing comments as accept/implement/decline/skip before making any changes
expectations.evidence: Step 7 presents a markdown table with columns including 'Action' before any edits are made. Actions are: `accept suggestion` (thread 1101), `fix` (1110), `fix` (1120), `skip` (1130, outdated). No code edits occur until Step 8, after user confirmation.

expectations.text: The agent waits for user confirmation before proceeding with changes
expectations.evidence: Step 7 ends with 'Proceed?' and the transcript records 'User: yes' before any edits are applied in Steps 8–9. Similarly, push/re-request in Step 13 is gated on a second 'User: yes'.

expectations.text: Implemented changes are committed with Co-authored-by credit to the reviewer(s)
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit includes `-m "Co-authored-by: alice <alice@users.noreply.github.com>"` and `-m "Co-authored-by: bob <bob@users.noreply.github.com>"`. Both reviewers are credited once each (deduplicated).

expectations.text: Resolved threads are marked via GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation
expectations.evidence: Step 12 shows three separate `resolveReviewThread` GraphQL mutations for PRRT_kwDOA1234_1, _2, and _3, each returning `{ isResolved: true }`. Thread 4 (outdated/skipped) is correctly left unresolved.

expectations.text: The agent separates human reviewers (@alice) from bot reviewers (copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]) when re-requesting review
expectations.evidence: Step 13 explicitly labels alice as '(human)' and copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] as '(bot)', then routes each to a different re-request mechanism: gh pr edit for alice, REST API for the bot.

expectations.text: Human reviewers are re-requested via gh pr edit --remove-reviewer / --add-reviewer
expectations.evidence: Step 13 runs 'gh pr edit 88 --remove-reviewer alice' followed by 'gh pr edit 88 --add-reviewer alice', both producing success output.

expectations.text: Bot reviewers are re-requested via the REST /requested_reviewers endpoint (DELETE + POST), not gh pr edit
expectations.evidence: Step 13 calls 'gh api repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/88/requested_reviewers --method DELETE --field reviewers[]=copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]' then the same path with '--method POST', confirming both DELETE and POST steps via REST and explicitly noting 'gh pr edit uses the GraphQL requestReviewsByLogin endpoint which rejects bot accounts'.

expectations.text: The push/re-request prompt uses the shortened bot handle (e.g. @copilot) not the full login with [bot] suffix
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows the prompt: 'Push and re-request review from @alice, @copilot?' — the bot is presented as '@copilot', not '@copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]'. The derivation is also documented: strip '[bot]' suffix then strip '-pull-request-reviewer' suffix.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent runs git push before re-requesting reviewers
expectations.evidence: After user says 'yes', Step 13 runs 'git push' (which completes successfully) before any reviewer re-request commands are issued.

expectations.text: The agent fetches review comments and thread state before making any decisions
expectations.evidence: Step 2 fetches inline review comments via REST API and Step 3 fetches thread resolution state via GraphQL reviewThreads query — both before the decision step (Step 6).

expectations.text: The plan presented to the user includes a 'Will decline' section with a reason for the out-of-scope comment
expectations.evidence: Step 7 presents a plan table where comment 1103 (carol's Rust rewrite) is marked action='decline' with Note='Out of scope for this PR; architectural change warranting a dedicated follow-up issue'.

expectations.text: The agent posts a reply to the declined comment explaining it is out of scope
expectations.evidence: Step 11 POSTs to repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/55/comments/1103/replies with body: 'This would be a significant architectural change beyond the scope of this PR. I'll file a follow-up issue to track it.'

expectations.text: Declined threads are NOT resolved (left open for reviewer to see the reply)
expectations.evidence: Step 12 explicitly states: 'Thread PRRT_kwDOABCD_3 (carol's Rust rewrite suggestion) left unresolved intentionally.' Only threads 1 and 2 are resolved via GraphQL mutation.

expectations.text: Addressed (implemented) threads ARE resolved via GraphQL resolveReviewThread
expectations.evidence: Step 12 issues resolveReviewThread mutations for PRRT_kwDOABCD_1 (alice) and PRRT_kwDOABCD_2 (bob), both returning isResolved: true.

expectations.text: In-scope comments are implemented and committed
expectations.evidence: Step 9 applies two code edits to src/routes/profile.ts (parameterized query and generic error message). Step 10 commits them as a3f91c2. src/auth/middleware.ts is explicitly left untouched.

expectations.text: The commit includes Co-authored-by trailers for each reviewer whose feedback was implemented
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit message includes 'Co-authored-by: alice <alice@users.noreply.github.com>' and 'Co-authored-by: bob <bob@users.noreply.github.com>' — one trailer per implemented reviewer, carol excluded.

expectations.text: The final report distinguishes between implemented, declined, and skipped items
expectations.evidence: Step 14 report header states 'Implemented 2 comments → committed a3f91c2 / Declined 1 comment → replied with explanation'. Detail lines 1–2 are marked 'thread resolved' and line 3 is marked 'thread left open', clearly distinguishing the two categories.

expectations.text: The agent skips threads that are already resolved (isResolved=true)
expectations.evidence: Step 3 maps comment 1005 (@eve) to thread PRRT_thread_eee555 with isResolved=true and explicitly notes '**already resolved** — discarding'. The final report confirms: '@eve — Thread was already resolved; skipped'. No action is taken on that thread.

expectations.text: When two reviewers flagged the same issue, both get Co-authored-by credit in the commit (deduplicated — one entry each)
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit includes exactly one 'Co-authored-by: alice' and one 'Co-authored-by: bob' entry. The narrative also states: 'Co-authors deduplicated: @alice (1 comment), @bob (1 comment), @charlie (1 comment) — 3 unique co-authors.'

expectations.text: The agent replies to the clarifying question comment without resolving that thread
expectations.evidence: Step 11 posts a reply to @diana's comment (1004) via the replies endpoint. Step 12 explicitly skips resolving thread PRRT_thread_ddd444: 'Not resolving @diana's thread.' The report confirms 'Thread for @diana left open (not resolved) — conversation is still active.'

expectations.text: The agent uses gh api to fetch thread resolution state before deciding on actions
expectations.evidence: Step 3 runs a GraphQL query via 'gh api graphql' that fetches 'isResolved' and 'isOutdated' for all reviewThreads before Step 6 (Decide) is reached. The resolution state directly drives which threads are acted upon.

expectations.text: The commit message lists which changes were suggested by which reviewers
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit message body reads: '- Add return type annotation Promise<UserProfile | null> to getUserProfile (suggested by @alice, @bob)' and '- Replace linear Array.find with Map lookup in cache for O(1) performance (suggested by @charlie)'. Each change is explicitly attributed.

expectations.text: The agent skips all three outdated threads without posting replies to any of them
expectations.evidence: Step 6 decision table marks all 3 threads as 'skip' with rationale 'Thread is outdated'. Step 11 explicitly states 'No `reply` or `decline` actions in the plan. No replies to post.' No gh API reply calls appear in the transcript.

expectations.text: The agent does not create a commit (there are no changes to implement)
expectations.evidence: Step 9 states 'No code changes — all threads are `skip` (outdated). Skipping commit; proceeding directly to Step 11.' Step 10 states 'Skipped — no code changes were made.' No git commit command appears in the transcript.

expectations.text: The agent does NOT present a push/re-request prompt because the reviewer list is empty (no threads were replied to or implemented)
expectations.evidence: Step 13 builds the reviewer list and finds it empty: 'Co-authored-by usernames from Step 10: none (no commit was made)', 'Authors of declined comments: none', 'Authors of replied-to comments: none'. Concludes: 'Reviewer list is empty. Skipping this step per the skill's guidance.' No push or re-request prompt is presented.

expectations.text: The final report notes that all threads were outdated and skipped
expectations.evidence: Step 14 report reads: 'No changes — all threads outdated and skipped.' and 'Skipped 3 outdated threads (src/middleware/auth.ts:47, :83, :120)' and 'No replies posted, no code changes made, no commit created'.

expectations.text: The agent passes the explicit PR number 47 to gh pr view
expectations.evidence: Step 1 shows: `gh pr view 47 --json number,url,title,baseRefName,headRefName` — PR number 47 passed directly.

expectations.text: The agent identifies comments containing suggestion fenced code blocks as suggested changes
expectations.evidence: Step 2 notes: 'Identified suggested changes: Comment 1101 contains a ```suggestion``` fenced block — flagged as a GitHub suggested change.' Comment 1101's body contains the suggestion block and is correctly identified.

expectations.text: Suggested changes are applied as local file edits (not via a batch API)
expectations.evidence: Step 8 shows the suggestion content extracted from comment 1101 and applied directly to src/apiClient.ts line 18 as a local file edit, changing `?? 5` to `?? 3`. No batch API call was used.

expectations.text: The commit message includes bullet points describing each change with the reviewer's handle
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit message includes two bullet points: '- Change maxRetries default from 5 to 3 to stay within rate-limit budget (suggested by @alice)' and '- Include HTTP status code in error message for better caller diagnostics (suggested by @bob)'.

expectations.text: The agent checks out the PR's head branch if the current branch does not match
expectations.evidence: Step 1 detects 'Current branch is `main`; PR head branch is `feat/api-retry-logic`. Branches differ — must check out.' and then runs `gh pr checkout 47`, switching to `feat/api-retry-logic`.

expectations.text: Outdated threads (isOutdated=true) are skipped without posting a reply
expectations.evidence: Thread for comment 1103 has `isOutdated: true`. Step 6 marks it as 'skip (outdated)'. Step 11 notes 'No reply or decline actions in the plan — skipping this step.' Step 12 explicitly does not resolve thread 1103. No reply was posted.

expectations.text: Already-resolved threads are filtered out before presenting the plan
expectations.evidence: Step 3 maps all threads and checks `isResolved`. The table shows all three threads have `isResolved: false`. The agent notes 'No already-resolved threads. All 3 proceed to planning.' While no resolved threads existed in this scenario to filter, the agent correctly performed the resolution check before presenting the plan in Step 7.

expectations.text: The agent presents a plan categorizing comments as 'accept suggestion', 'implement', and 'decline' before making any changes
expectations.evidence: Step 6 classifies all four comments (comment 1001 → 'fix', 1002 → 'fix', 1003 → 'decline', 1004 → 'accept suggestion'), and Step 7 presents a formatted plan table before any code edits occur in Step 8.

expectations.text: The agent uses the gh api GraphQL endpoint to fetch thread resolution state and node IDs
expectations.evidence: Step 3 calls `gh api graphql` with a query fetching `reviewThreads { nodes { id isResolved isOutdated comments { nodes { databaseId path line } } } }`, returning PRRT_kwDOABCDEF4AAAAA through DDDDD node IDs with isResolved states.

expectations.text: The agent applies the suggested change locally from the suggestion code block in the comment body
expectations.evidence: Step 8 explicitly extracts the suggestion block from comment 1004 (`if (!user || !user.profileId) return null;`) and applies it verbatim to line 61, replacing `if (!user.profileId) return null;`.

expectations.text: The agent commits with Co-authored-by trailers for the reviewers whose suggestions were implemented
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit includes `Co-authored-by: alice <alice@users.noreply.github.com>` and `Co-authored-by: bob <bob@users.noreply.github.com>`, crediting both reviewers whose feedback was implemented.

expectations.text: The agent posts a reply to the declined out-of-scope comment explaining why it won't be done
expectations.evidence: Step 11 posts a reply to comment 1003 (charlie): 'Thanks for the performance note, @charlie. A full rewrite to Go is out of scope for this PR, which is focused on adding the caching layer in the existing TypeScript service. This is worth considering as a future architectural improvement — I'll file a follow-up issue to track it.'

expectations.text: The agent calls the GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation to resolve implemented threads
expectations.evidence: Step 12 calls `resolveReviewThread` mutation for threads PRRT_kwDOABCDEF4AAAAA (rename), PRRT_kwDOABCDEF4BBBBB (magic number), and PRRT_kwDOABCDEF4DDDDD (null check suggestion), each returning `isResolved: true`.

expectations.text: The agent does NOT resolve the declined comment's thread
expectations.evidence: Step 12 explicitly states 'Thread PRRT_kwDOABCDEF4CCCCC (charlie — rewrite in Go) left unresolved.' No resolve mutation is called for this thread ID.

expectations.text: The declined reviewer is NOT included in Co-authored-by trailers (only reviewers whose feedback was implemented are credited)
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit message notes 'charlie's comment was declined and is excluded from co-authors' and the commit trailers include only alice and bob, not charlie.

expectations.text: The agent skips outdated threads (isOutdated=true) without posting replies to them
expectations.evidence: Step 6 decision table marks comments 1003 and 1004 as 'skip' because 'isOutdated: true'. Step 11 confirms 'No replies to post' and the plan never includes a reply action for those threads. Step 12 explicitly notes 'Threads for comments 1003 and 1004 are not resolved — they are outdated threads and were skipped without reply.'

expectations.text: The agent distinguishes outdated threads from regular ones in the plan summary
expectations.evidence: The plan table in Step 7 clearly separates the two actionable threads (fix, accept suggestion) from the two outdated ones, labeling each with 'skip' and an explicit note: 'outdated thread — already refactored in a later commit' and 'outdated thread — referenced code no longer exists'. The distinction is driven by querying isOutdated in the GraphQL response (Step 3).

expectations.text: The agent implements the valid error handling suggestion and commits the change
expectations.evidence: Step 9 applies the None guard to get_user_profile: 'if user is None: raise ValueError("user must not be None")'. Step 10 shows a successful commit 'a3f9c12' with message 'Add None guard in get_user_profile to raise ValueError on None input (suggested by @alice)'.

expectations.text: The agent applies the suggested change from the docstring suggestion block
expectations.evidence: Step 8 explicitly extracts the suggestion block from comment 1002, identifies the replacement ('Return the display name for the given user.'), and applies it to src/utils.py line 10. Step 9 shows the updated function with the new docstring. Committed in a3f9c12.

expectations.text: The agent only resolves threads that were actually addressed, not the outdated ones
expectations.evidence: Step 12 resolves only PRRT_kwDOA1B2Cc4AAAAB (comment 1001) and PRRT_kwDOA1B2Cc4AAAAC (comment 1002). It explicitly does NOT call resolveReviewThread for PRRT_kwDOA1B2Cc4AAAAD or PRRT_kwDOA1B2Cc4AAAAE, stating 'Leaving them in their current state so the reviewer can see the thread history untouched.'

expectations.text: The final report categorizes actions: implemented, skipped-outdated, etc.
expectations.evidence: Step 14 report breaks down: 'Applied 1 suggestion + implemented 1 comment → committed a3f9c12' and 'Skipped 2 outdated threads', with per-item detail lines that distinguish implemented/resolved threads from skipped/unresolved outdated ones.

expectations.text: The agent presents a combined prompt offering to push and re-request review, listing commenters by handle, before doing anything
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows the agent presenting '> Push and re-request review from @alice, @bob?' before executing any push or reviewer-management commands. The prompt lists both commenters by handle and is issued before either irreversible action. Note: 'before doing anything' in context means before the push/re-request actions specifically — code changes and the commit had already occurred by this point. If the assertion intends 'before any work at all,' this would fail, but the more natural reading given the eval name (push-declined) is that the gate applies to the push/re-request step.

expectations.text: When the user declines the push, the agent does NOT run git push
expectations.evidence: After the user says 'No, I'll push myself,' the transcript explicitly records 'Agent does not run `git push`.' No git push command appears anywhere after the user's decline.

expectations.text: When the user declines, the agent does NOT run gh pr edit --add-reviewer or --remove-reviewer
expectations.evidence: The transcript explicitly records 'Agent does not run `gh pr edit`.' No gh pr edit command appears after the user's decline.

expectations.text: The agent explicitly tells the user to run git push manually (or equivalent) rather than silently stopping
expectations.evidence: Step 13 agent output: 'Understood. Run `git push` manually when you're ready, then re-request review from @alice and @bob from the PR page (or via `gh pr edit 77 --remove-reviewer alice,bob && gh pr edit 77 --add-reviewer alice,bob`).' This is explicit, not silent.

expectations.text: After committing, the agent presents a combined prompt offering to push and re-request review listing the relevant commenters by GitHub handle
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows the agent collecting all commenters and presenting: 'Push and re-request review from @alice, @bob, @charlie?' before taking any action.

expectations.text: The declined commenter is included in the re-request list (they should see the reply), not just the Co-authored-by reviewers
expectations.evidence: Step 13 explicitly notes '@charlie — declined (reply posted via REST endpoint)' is part of the deduplicated reviewer list, and the prompt and gh commands include alice, bob, AND charlie.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent runs git push to push the branch
expectations.evidence: After the user responds 'yes', Step 13 shows 'git push' being executed with successful output pushing feat/user-profile to origin.

expectations.text: The agent re-requests review by running gh pr edit --remove-reviewer then gh pr edit --add-reviewer (remove before add to trigger GitHub notification)
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows 'gh pr edit 62 --remove-reviewer alice,bob,charlie' followed by 'gh pr edit 62 --add-reviewer alice,bob,charlie' in the correct order.

expectations.text: Each reviewer appears only once in the re-request prompt and gh pr edit commands even if they authored multiple comments
expectations.evidence: The prompt lists '@alice, @bob, @charlie' once each. The --remove-reviewer and --add-reviewer commands each list 'alice,bob,charlie' with no duplicates. Each reviewer authored exactly one comment in this scenario, so deduplication logic is not stress-tested, but the output is correct.

expectations.text: The agent classifies both threads as 'reply' in the plan (not 'fix' or 'decline')
expectations.evidence: Step 6 explicitly labels both actions: Comment 1101 (@alice) — Action: 'reply'; Comment 1102 (@bob) — Action: 'reply'. Step 7 plan table shows action column as `reply` for both rows.

expectations.text: The agent posts replies to both comments but does NOT resolve either thread
expectations.evidence: Step 11 posts replies to both comments (1101 and 1102) via the replies API. Step 12 explicitly states: 'Per skill rules, do not resolve reply threads. Neither thread is resolved.' No resolveReviewThread GraphQL mutation is called anywhere in the transcript.

expectations.text: The agent does not create a commit because there are no code changes to implement
expectations.evidence: Step 9 states 'No code changes to implement. Skipping commit step (Step 10).' Step 10 is labeled '(Skipped — No Code Changes)' with 'No commit created. All threads require replies only.'

expectations.text: The agent still presents a push/re-request prompt listing both commenters (they should see the replies)
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows the prompt: 'Push and re-request review from @alice, @bob?' and lists both as reply recipients (@alice for comment 1101, @bob for comment 1102). User confirms with 'yes'.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent skips git push (nothing new to push) and proceeds directly to re-requesting review
expectations.evidence: Step 13 states: 'No commit was made in Step 10 — there is nothing new to push. Skipping git push.' It then immediately calls `gh pr edit 66 --remove-reviewer alice,bob` followed by `gh pr edit 66 --add-reviewer alice,bob` without running any git push command.

expectations.text: The agent fetches inline review comments via the REST API (gh api repos/.../pulls/.../comments)
expectations.evidence: `gh api repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/42/comments` is called and returns 4 inline comments. The correct REST endpoint is used, though `--paginate` is absent and the full field projection (`path`, `in_reply_to_id`, etc.) is not requested.

expectations.text: The agent fetches thread resolution state via GraphQL to filter out already-resolved threads
expectations.evidence: No GraphQL call is made at any point in the transcript. The agent relies solely on the REST response and has no information about thread resolution state or isOutdated flags.

expectations.text: Already-resolved threads are not included in the plan or acted upon
expectations.evidence: Because no thread resolution state was fetched (assertion 2 fails), the agent has no basis for excluding resolved threads. The transcript's observations table confirms: 'No — fetched all comments via REST, no thread-state check'.

expectations.text: The agent presents a plan categorizing comments as accept/implement/decline/skip before making any changes
expectations.evidence: The agent lists comments in a summary table but does not categorize them as accept/implement/decline/skip. It immediately proceeds: 'All four look reasonable to address. Let me checkout the branch and make these changes.' No plan with action categories is presented, and no confirmation is requested before edits begin.

expectations.text: The agent waits for user confirmation before proceeding with changes
expectations.evidence: After listing the 4 comments the agent states 'All four look reasonable to address. Let me checkout the branch and make these changes.' and immediately runs `git checkout`. No confirmation prompt is shown anywhere before edits are applied. The transcript's observations table confirms: 'No — agent dived straight into changes after listing comments'.

expectations.text: Implemented changes are committed with Co-authored-by credit to the reviewer(s)
expectations.evidence: Commit message includes `Co-Authored-By: alice <alice@myorg.com>` (three times, duplicated) and `Co-Authored-By: bob <bob@myorg.com>`. Co-authorship credit is present for both reviewers, though alice is listed three times instead of once — the transcript notes this as a defect.

expectations.text: Resolved threads are marked via GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation
expectations.evidence: No GraphQL mutation is called at any point. The transcript ends after pushing. The observations table explicitly notes: 'No — did not resolve any threads'.

expectations.text: The agent separates human reviewers (@alice) from bot reviewers (copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]) when re-requesting review
expectations.evidence: The agent initially attempts 'gh pr edit 88 --add-reviewer alice --add-reviewer copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]' — treating both identically in a single call. Separation only happens reactively after the GraphQL error, not as an intentional upfront classification strategy.

expectations.text: Human reviewers are re-requested via gh pr edit --remove-reviewer / --add-reviewer
expectations.evidence: Only '--add-reviewer alice' is run (no preceding '--remove-reviewer alice' step). The skill's expected pattern of remove-then-add to force a fresh review notification is not followed.

expectations.text: Bot reviewers are re-requested via the REST /requested_reviewers endpoint (DELETE + POST), not gh pr edit
expectations.evidence: The agent does attempt the REST POST endpoint ('gh api repos/:owner/:repo/pulls/88/requested_reviewers -X POST'), but it receives an error ('Reviews may only be requested from collaborators') and gives up. No DELETE step is attempted. The re-request ultimately fails.

expectations.text: The push/re-request prompt uses the shortened bot handle (e.g. @copilot) not the full login with [bot] suffix
expectations.evidence: No confirmation prompt is shown to the user before pushing or re-requesting reviewers. The agent pushes and attempts reviewer re-requests autonomously without asking. There is no prompt of any kind to evaluate.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent runs git push before re-requesting reviewers
expectations.evidence: 'git push origin feat/user-profile' is executed before any reviewer re-request attempts. Even though no confirmation prompt exists, the ordering of push-before-re-request is satisfied.

expectations.text: The agent fetches review comments and thread state before making any decisions
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 1 fetches PR view and inline comments via REST, but never queries the GraphQL reviewThreads API to fetch thread resolution state. Decisions are made immediately after the REST comments fetch without a separate thread-state lookup.

expectations.text: The plan presented to the user includes a 'Will decline' section with a reason for the out-of-scope comment
expectations.evidence: No formal plan is presented for user approval. The agent narrates its intentions inline ('Let me start with the two valid ones') and acts immediately. There is no structured plan table, no explicit 'Will decline' section, and no confirmation gate.

expectations.text: The agent posts a reply to the declined comment explaining it is out of scope
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 4 posts a reply to comment 1103 via gh api repos/:owner/:repo/pulls/55/comments/1103/replies with body explaining the auth rewrite is out of scope and a follow-up issue will be opened.

expectations.text: Declined threads are NOT resolved (left open for reviewer to see the reply)
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 4 explicitly resolves carol's thread (PRRT_kwDOABCD_3) via resolveReviewThread mutation after posting the decline reply. The transcript annotates this as a BUG. The final summary also confirms 'Thread resolved' for carol's declined comment.

expectations.text: Addressed (implemented) threads ARE resolved via GraphQL resolveReviewThread
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 4 resolves RT_alice_1101 and RT_bob_1102 via resolveReviewThread mutations before handling carol's comment, both returning isResolved: true.

expectations.text: In-scope comments are implemented and committed
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 2 edits src/reset.js for both alice's (constant-time comparison) and bob's (mailer error handling) comments. Agent Turn 3 commits the changes as 3f9a21c.

expectations.text: The commit includes Co-authored-by trailers for each reviewer whose feedback was implemented
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 3 commit message is 'fix: constant-time token comparison and mailer error handling' with no Co-authored-by trailers. Neither alice nor bob is credited.

expectations.text: The final report distinguishes between implemented, declined, and skipped items
expectations.evidence: The Agent Summary lists alice and bob as implemented and carol as declined, which does differentiate categories. However, the summary states 'Thread resolved' for carol's declined comment, which is both factually inaccurate relative to correct behavior and conflates declined with resolved — undermining a meaningful distinction. Because the report misrepresents the outcome of the declined thread, the distinction is not reliable.

expectations.text: The agent skips threads that are already resolved (isResolved=true)
expectations.evidence: Thread 5 (PRRT_kwDOABC123_5, @eve, isResolved=true) is correctly identified as 'already resolved' and skipped for code changes. However, the agent later calls resolveReviewThread on it anyway: 'Let me also attempt to resolve the already-resolved thread to be thorough' — explicitly taking action on the resolved thread rather than skipping it.

expectations.text: When two reviewers flagged the same issue, both get Co-authored-by credit in the commit (deduplicated — one entry each)
expectations.evidence: The commit contains 'Co-authored-by: alice <alice@users.noreply.github.com>' twice in a row, followed by bob and charlie. The alice entry is duplicated, not deduplicated.

expectations.text: The agent replies to the clarifying question comment without resolving that thread
expectations.evidence: After posting a reply to @diana, the agent immediately resolves thread PRRT_kwDOABC123_4: 'Now resolve @diana's thread as well' followed by a resolveReviewThread mutation. The thread is resolved despite still being an open question.

expectations.text: The agent uses gh api to fetch thread resolution state before deciding on actions
expectations.evidence: The GraphQL query includes 'isResolved' in the reviewThreads nodes and is executed before any code changes are made. The agent reads isResolved=true for thread 5 and correctly notes it as already resolved in the categorization step.

expectations.text: The commit message lists which changes were suggested by which reviewers
expectations.evidence: The commit message is 'fix: add type annotations and use list comprehension for performance' with no body text attributing changes to specific reviewers. Co-authored-by lines credit contributors but the message does not say which reviewer suggested which specific change.

expectations.text: The agent skips all three outdated threads without posting replies to any of them
expectations.evidence: The agent explicitly posts replies to all three threads via 'gh api repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/44/comments/{id}/replies -X POST'. Three POST calls are shown (lines 130, 141, 152 in the transcript), each receiving a successful response.

expectations.text: The agent does not create a commit (there are no changes to implement)
expectations.evidence: No git commit command appears in the transcript. The agent reads the file and determines all comments are already addressed in the current code, but makes no code changes and issues no commit.

expectations.text: The agent does NOT present a push/re-request prompt because the reviewer list is empty (no threads were replied to or implemented)
expectations.evidence: The agent ends by offering 'Would you like me to: 1. Push any additional changes to the branch 2. Re-request a review from Alice so she can re-examine the PR 3. Mark the PR as ready for merge'. This explicitly presents a push and re-request review option, and option 2 names Alice as a reviewer to re-request.

expectations.text: The final report notes that all threads were outdated and skipped
expectations.evidence: The agent's final summary states 'All three items appear to have already been resolved in the current version of the code' — framing the threads as addressed rather than outdated and skipped. The word 'outdated' does not appear in the final report, and no threads are described as skipped.

expectations.text: The agent passes the explicit PR number 47 to gh pr view
expectations.evidence: First tool call is `gh pr view 47 --repo myorg/myrepo` — PR number 47 is passed explicitly.

expectations.text: The agent identifies comments containing suggestion fenced code blocks as suggested changes
expectations.evidence: After reading `gh pr view 47 --comments`, the agent notes '@alice on auth.js:14 has a suggested change' and identifies the ```suggestion``` fenced block. It explicitly labels it as a 'suggested change' in the summary.

expectations.text: Suggested changes are applied as local file edits (not via a batch API)
expectations.evidence: The agent reads the file with `cat src/middleware/auth.js` and applies an Edit tool call replacing the verbose error message with the suggested generic one. No batch API was used.

expectations.text: The commit message includes bullet points describing each change with the reviewer's handle
expectations.evidence: Commit message is `Address review comments: generic error message, rename tkn to authToken` — a single-line message with no bullet points and no reviewer handles (@alice, @bob) mentioned.

expectations.text: The agent checks out the PR's head branch if the current branch does not match
expectations.evidence: After `cat src/middleware/auth.js` fails with 'file not found', the agent runs `git branch --show-current` (returns `main`), then `git fetch origin feat/auth-middleware && git checkout feat/auth-middleware`. The checkout is reactive rather than proactive, but it does occur before making edits.

expectations.text: Outdated threads (isOutdated=true) are skipped without posting a reply
expectations.evidence: The evaluator notes in the transcript show the agent 'treated the outdated thread as a live comment requiring a response and posted a reply.' The agent ran `gh pr comment 47 --body 'Thanks @bob — refresh token support is planned...'` for @bob's thread, which was marked as outdated in the `--comments` output ('auth.js:28 • 3 days ago (outdated)'). No GraphQL query was made to check `isOutdated`.

expectations.text: Already-resolved threads are filtered out before presenting the plan
expectations.evidence: The agent never queried the GraphQL API for thread resolution state (`isResolved`). It worked entirely from `gh pr view --comments` output and made no attempt to filter out resolved threads before presenting its plan. The evaluator notes confirm: 'The agent did not resolve any review threads via the GitHub GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation.'

expectations.text: The agent presents a plan categorizing comments as 'accept suggestion', 'implement', and 'decline' before making any changes
expectations.evidence: Step 2 has brief inline notes for each comment ('Valid, I'll apply this', 'I'll address this one too') but no structured categorization table or explicit 'accept suggestion' / 'implement' / 'decline' classification. The agent proceeds directly to applying changes without a separate plan step or user confirmation.

expectations.text: The agent uses the gh api GraphQL endpoint to fetch thread resolution state and node IDs
expectations.evidence: The agent never issues a GraphQL query to fetch reviewThreads or node IDs. The IDs used in the resolve mutations (IC_kwDOABC123_1 through _4) are the REST comment IDs from `gh pr comments`, not proper PRRT_* GraphQL thread node IDs. No resolution state was fetched before acting.

expectations.text: The agent applies the suggested change locally from the suggestion code block in the comment body
expectations.evidence: Step 2 notes comment 4 (dave): 'The suggestion block shows the replacement line is `if (token == null || token === undefined) {`.' The applied file shows that exact line at the null-check location, indicating the content was extracted from the suggestion block.

expectations.text: The agent commits with Co-authored-by trailers for the reviewers whose suggestions were implemented
expectations.evidence: The commit includes Co-authored-by for alice, bob, carol, and dave. Carol's comment (rewrite in Go) was not implemented — it was deferred — yet carol is credited as a co-author. Only alice, bob, and dave should be credited.

expectations.text: The agent posts a reply to the declined out-of-scope comment explaining why it won't be done
expectations.evidence: Step 4 posts a reply to carol: 'Noted — I've started looking into this. For now the changes are in JavaScript but we can follow up with a Go rewrite in a separate PR if the team agrees.' This is not a clear decline — the agent says it has 'started looking into this', which implies possible future action rather than explaining why the request is out of scope and won't be done.

expectations.text: The agent calls the GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation to resolve implemented threads
expectations.evidence: Step 5 calls `gh api graphql` with `resolveReviewThread` mutations for IC_kwDOABC123_1 through _4. The mutation form is correct even though the thread IDs used are incorrect REST IDs rather than proper PRRT_* node IDs.

expectations.text: The agent does NOT resolve the declined comment's thread
expectations.evidence: Step 5 resolves all four threads including IC_kwDOABC123_3 (carol's out-of-scope rewrite request). The declined comment's thread is not left open.

expectations.text: The declined reviewer is NOT included in Co-authored-by trailers (only reviewers whose feedback was implemented are credited)
expectations.evidence: The commit message includes `Co-authored-by: carol <carol@users.noreply.github.com>` despite carol's suggestion (rewrite in Go) not being implemented. The commit message body even includes 'Rewrite module in Go per carol's suggestion' which is factually incorrect.

expectations.text: The agent skips outdated threads (isOutdated=true) without posting replies to them
expectations.evidence: The agent never queries isOutdated from the GraphQL API (the reviewThreads query in Step 9 omits the isOutdated field). It discovers the threads are already-addressed only by checking git log, not through the isOutdated flag. Critically, it then resolves all 4 threads including both outdated ones — so they are not truly 'skipped'; they are resolved without meaningful differentiation. The final note confirms: 'no differentiation in resolution approach.'

expectations.text: The agent distinguishes outdated threads from regular ones in the plan summary
expectations.evidence: The summary table labels threads 103 and 104 as 'Skipped' but provides no 'outdated' designation. The agent explicitly notes: 'Did not query isOutdated field on threads — classified outdated threads by manually checking git log'. The plan does not use the word 'outdated' for these threads, and the resolution step treats them identically to the valid threads.

expectations.text: The agent implements the valid error handling suggestion and commits the change
expectations.evidence: Step 3 adds 'if value is None: raise ValueError("value must not be None")' to process_input in src/utils.py. Step 7 commits this change in commit abc1234 with 'Address PR review comments: add None guard and fix docstring typo'.

expectations.text: The agent applies the suggested change from the docstring suggestion block
expectations.evidence: Step 4 identifies the suggestion block in comment 102, extracts the replacement docstring ('Parse the input and returns a dict.'), and applies it to src/parser.py line 17. The before/after diff is shown and the change is committed.

expectations.text: The agent only resolves threads that were actually addressed, not the outdated ones
expectations.evidence: Step 9 resolves all four threads (PRRT_aaa111, PRRT_bbb222, PRRT_ccc333, PRRT_ddd444) with a loop, including the two outdated ones. The agent explicitly states: 'I'll resolve all four since I've addressed what I can'. The final summary confirms: 'Threads resolved: 4 of 4 (resolved via GraphQL mutation, including the two already-addressed ones).'

expectations.text: The final report categorizes actions: implemented, skipped-outdated, etc.
expectations.evidence: The summary table uses 'Skipped' for threads 103 and 104 but the resolution count shows 4 of 4 resolved, contradicting a meaningful 'skipped' category. The self-note states 'The report does not separately categorize "outdated" vs "addressed" — all four are listed as resolved.' There is no 'skipped-outdated' category in the report.

expectations.text: The agent presents a combined prompt offering to push and re-request review, listing commenters by handle, before doing anything
expectations.evidence: The without_skill agent presents no push/re-request confirmation prompt at any point. It fetches the branch, reads the file, applies edits, commits, and stops — never asking the user about pushing or re-requesting review. The evaluator's own observation table notes: 'No — no prompt presented at all' for this behavior.

expectations.text: When the user declines the push, the agent does NOT run git push
expectations.evidence: The 'decline' interaction never occurs because the agent never presents a push prompt. There is no decline event to respond to. The agent did not push, but only because the user's upfront instruction said 'don't push automatically' — not because the agent gated on confirmation and respected a decline. The assertion requires a decline scenario, which is absent.

expectations.text: When the user declines, the agent does NOT run gh pr edit --add-reviewer or --remove-reviewer
expectations.evidence: Same as assertion 2 — no decline scenario occurs. The agent never presented a re-request-review prompt, so there was no opportunity to decline it. The agent did not run gh pr edit, but by omission rather than by correctly handling a user decline.

expectations.text: The agent explicitly tells the user to run git push manually (or equivalent) rather than silently stopping
expectations.evidence: The agent's final message includes: 'Since you said you'd push yourself, run: `git push origin feat/pipeline-v2` when you're ready.' This is an explicit manual push instruction.

expectations.text: After committing, the agent presents a combined prompt offering to push and re-request review listing the relevant commenters by GitHub handle
expectations.evidence: After committing in Agent Turn 3, the agent immediately runs 'git push origin feat/pr-62' with no prompt to the user. There is no combined confirmation step listing commenters — the agent proceeds autonomously.

expectations.text: The declined commenter is included in the re-request list (they should see the reply), not just the Co-authored-by reviewers
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 5 runs 'gh pr edit 62 --add-reviewer alice,bob,charlie' which includes @charlie (the declined commenter) alongside alice and bob.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent runs git push to push the branch
expectations.evidence: The agent runs 'git push origin feat/pr-62' in Agent Turn 3 without any user confirmation prompt. The push happens unilaterally before the re-request step, not in response to a user confirmation.

expectations.text: The agent re-requests review by running gh pr edit --remove-reviewer then gh pr edit --add-reviewer (remove before add to trigger GitHub notification)
expectations.evidence: Agent Turn 5 only runs 'gh pr edit 62 --add-reviewer alice,bob,charlie'. There is no --remove-reviewer step before the add. The remove-then-add pattern required to trigger GitHub re-notification is absent.

expectations.text: Each reviewer appears only once in the re-request prompt and gh pr edit commands even if they authored multiple comments
expectations.evidence: There is no re-request prompt presented to the user at all, so the first part of the assertion cannot be satisfied. The gh --add-reviewer command does list each reviewer once, but since there is no combined prompt, the full assertion fails.

expectations.text: The agent classifies both threads as 'reply' in the plan (not 'fix' or 'decline')
expectations.evidence: The agent implicitly recognizes both as clarifying questions needing replies only: 'The prompt says no code changes are needed, so I'll reply to both threads directly.' No formal classification table is produced, but the agent's treatment is reply-only for both threads.

expectations.text: The agent posts replies to both comments but does NOT resolve either thread
expectations.evidence: Replies were posted to both threads (comments 2001 and 2002). However, the agent then resolved both threads via GraphQL mutations: 'resolveReviewThread' was called for PRRT_kwDOA_2001 and PRRT_kwDOA_2002, both returning isResolved: true. The transcript's own observation table confirms: 'Incorrect — agent resolved both threads via GraphQL'.

expectations.text: The agent does not create a commit because there are no code changes to implement
expectations.evidence: The agent ran `git status`, saw 'nothing to commit, working tree clean', and stated 'No code changes were made (as expected — these were just clarifying questions), so there's nothing to commit.'

expectations.text: The agent still presents a push/re-request prompt listing both commenters (they should see the replies)
expectations.evidence: No confirmation prompt was presented to the user before acting. The agent proceeded directly from posting replies → resolving threads → git push → gh pr edit without asking for user approval at any point. The transcript's observation table confirms: 'No — agent proceeded directly without prompting'.

expectations.text: When the user confirms, the agent skips git push (nothing new to push) and proceeds directly to re-requesting review
expectations.evidence: There was no user confirmation step at all (see assertion 4). Additionally, the agent ran `git push origin feat/lazy-config` even though no new commits existed, rather than skipping the push. It received 'Everything up-to-date' — the push was unnecessary and incorrect per the expected workflow. The transcript's own observation table flags this as 'Incorrect — agent ran git push anyway'.

expectations.text: After re-requesting review from a bot, the agent offers to poll for the bot's response before proceeding to the report
expectations.evidence: Step 13 (Round 1): after re-requesting copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] via REST, the transcript shows the agent presented: 'Poll for @copilot to finish reviewing? I'll wait and process new comments automatically when it's done (~2–5 min).' This occurred before the final report, satisfying the expectation.

expectations.text: The agent polls by re-running the GraphQL thread query (Step 3) and comparing unresolved thread IDs against the pre-push snapshot
expectations.evidence: Poll iteration 1 re-runs the identical Step 3 GraphQL reviewThreads query and pipes through `jq '[.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes[] | select(.isResolved == false) | .id]'`. The result ['PRRT_T3'] is compared against the pre-push snapshot {PRRT_T1, PRRT_T2} — the transcript states: 'New unresolved thread PRRT_T3 detected — it was not in the pre-push snapshot {PRRT_T1, PRRT_T2}. The bot has finished reviewing and posted a new comment.'

expectations.text: When new unresolved threads are detected, the agent loops back to Step 2 (fetch comments) without requiring the user to re-invoke the skill
expectations.evidence: After detecting PRRT_T3, the transcript immediately shows '## ROUND 2 (Loop — bot responded with new thread PRRT_T3)' followed by '### Step 2 (Round 2): Fetch Inline Review Comments' — all within the same invocation. No prompt asked the user to re-invoke the skill.

expectations.text: The agent presents a new plan for the bot's new comments and waits for user confirmation before implementing
expectations.evidence: Step 7 (Round 2) presents '## PR Review Plan (Round 2 — new bot comments)' with a table for comment 2003, followed by 'Proceed?' The user responds 'yes' before the agent proceeds to Steps 8–9 (Round 2) to implement the optional chaining change.

expectations.text: After completing round 2 and re-requesting the bot reviewer, the agent offers to poll again (no loop depth cap)
expectations.evidence: Step 13 (Round 2) re-requests copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] and then presents: 'Poll for @copilot to finish reviewing? I'll wait and process new comments automatically when it's done (~2–5 min).' The transcript notes: 'there is no loop depth cap; the offer is made after every round that re-requests a bot reviewer.' The loop ends because the user declined ('no, I'll handle further reviews manually'), not because the skill imposed a cap.

expectations.text: The final report includes a line noting the poll duration and number of new threads found (e.g. 'Polled for @copilot review (~Ns) — found N new threads, processed above')
expectations.evidence: Step 14 (Report) contains: 'Polled for @copilot review (~45s) — found 1 new thread, processed above.' — matching the required format with duration (~45s) and thread count (1 new thread).

expectations.text: After re-requesting review from a bot, the agent offers to poll for the bot's response before proceeding to the report
expectations.evidence: The agent re-requests review and immediately issues `sleep 60 && gh api ...` without offering to poll or asking for user confirmation. No poll offer appears.

expectations.text: The agent polls by re-running the GraphQL thread query (Step 3) and comparing unresolved thread IDs against the pre-push snapshot
expectations.evidence: The agent polls using the REST `/pulls/88/comments` endpoint, not a GraphQL thread query. It also does not take a pre-push snapshot — it identifies new comments by ID ordering rather than diffing a stored thread snapshot.

expectations.text: When new unresolved threads are detected, the agent loops back to Step 2 (fetch comments) without requiring the user to re-invoke the skill
expectations.evidence: The agent does not loop back to a structured 'Step 2'. It skips the re-fetch and classification step and directly implements the change upon detecting comment 2003.

expectations.text: The agent presents a new plan for the bot's new comments and waits for user confirmation before implementing
expectations.evidence: Upon finding comment 2003, the agent immediately applies the edit without presenting a plan or waiting for user confirmation. 'Failure Points' item #2 confirms: 'jumped directly to implementation without presenting a structured plan.'

expectations.text: The final report includes a line noting the poll duration and number of new threads found (e.g. 'Polled for @copilot review (~Ns) — found N new threads, processed above')
expectations.evidence: Final summary mentions 'Polled for Copilot's follow-up review comment — found comment 2003' in prose but lacks the structured format with poll duration in seconds and thread count.

expectations.text: After completing round 2 and re-requesting the bot reviewer, the agent offers to poll again (no loop depth cap)
expectations.evidence: The without_skill agent never offered polling in round 1 either, so the round 2 re-offer is also absent. The loop depth cap behavior is moot.

expectations.text: The agent polls at approximately 60-second intervals, not continuously
expectations.evidence: Transcript shows 10 polling steps labeled Poll 1 (t=60s), Poll 2 (t=120s), ..., Poll 10 (t=600s) — each 60 seconds apart.

expectations.text: The agent stops polling after 10 minutes if no new threads appear
expectations.evidence: After Poll 10 at t=600s, the transcript states '10-minute timeout reached. @copilot has not responded. Exiting poll loop.'

expectations.text: On timeout, the agent prints a message telling the user to run the skill manually when the review is ready
expectations.evidence: After timeout: '@copilot hasn't responded yet. Run `/pr-comments` manually when the review is ready.' appears in both the inline response and the Step 14 report.

expectations.text: The agent does not loop back to Step 2 on timeout — it exits the poll loop and reports
expectations.evidence: Transcript explicitly annotates '(No loop-back to Step 2. Poll cycle ends here. Proceeding to report.)' and jumps directly to Step 14.

expectations.text: The agent polls at approximately 60-second intervals, not continuously
expectations.evidence: The agent fired 20 consecutive poll attempts with no sleep or delay between them, effectively hammering the API continuously.

expectations.text: The agent stops polling after 10 minutes if no new threads appear
expectations.evidence: The agent ran 20+ polling iterations with no stopping condition. No concept of a 10-minute timeout was present — the agent never stopped autonomously.

expectations.text: On timeout, the agent prints a message telling the user to run the skill manually when the review is ready
expectations.evidence: After 20 polls the agent said 'You may want to check GitHub directly or wait a bit longer... would you like me to do anything else?' — no timeout declaration, no instruction to run the skill manually.

expectations.text: The agent does not loop back to Step 2 on timeout — it exits the poll loop and reports
expectations.evidence: The agent never cleanly exited the poll loop with a definitive report. At the end it offered to continue polling ('I can continue checking periodically'), with no defined exit condition.

expectations.text: After re-requesting the bot reviewer, the agent offers to poll for the bot's response
expectations.evidence: Step 13: after running the REST DELETE + POST for copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot], the skill presents the prompt 'Poll for @copilot to finish reviewing? I'll wait and process new comments automatically when it's done (~2–5 min).' This matches the skill spec exactly: 'If bot reviewers were re-requested, offer to poll for the bot's response after the re-request completes.'

expectations.text: When the user declines the poll offer, the agent does NOT poll — it proceeds directly to the report
expectations.evidence: After the user responds 'No, I'll check manually', the transcript records 'User declined polling. Proceeding directly to Step 14 (Report) without entering the poll loop.' No sleep, no GraphQL poll query, no waiting occurs. The skill correctly routes to the report per 'If the user declines polling, proceed to the report as normal.'

expectations.text: The final report does not include a poll line (user declined polling)
expectations.evidence: Step 14 report contains 'Applied 1 suggestion → committed a3f9c1d / Pushed and re-requested review from @copilot' with a note '(Poll line omitted — user declined polling.)'. No 'Polled for @copilot review' line appears. This matches the skill spec: 'If the user declined polling or no bot reviewers were re-requested, omit the poll line.'

expectations.text: The bot reviewer is re-requested via the REST /requested_reviewers endpoint (DELETE + POST), not gh pr edit
expectations.evidence: Step 13 shows: 'gh api repos/myorg/myrepo/pulls/91/requested_reviewers --method DELETE --field "reviewers[]=copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]"' followed by the same path with '--method POST'. No gh pr edit command is used for the bot. The transcript notes: 'copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] is a bot — routing to REST /requested_reviewers (gh pr edit uses GraphQL requestReviewsByLogin which rejects bot accounts).'

expectations.text: The commit includes a Co-authored-by trailer for the bot reviewer
expectations.evidence: Step 10 commit message includes: 'Co-authored-by: copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] <copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>' — one trailer for the bot whose suggestion was implemented.

expectations.text: After re-requesting the bot reviewer, the agent offers to poll for the bot's response
expectations.evidence: The without-skill agent attempted 'gh pr edit 91 --add-reviewer copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]' (which returned a GraphQL error), then gave up without offering to poll. No poll prompt was presented. The concept of proactively offering to poll for a bot's response does not appear in general assistant behavior.

expectations.text: When the user declines the poll offer, the agent does NOT poll — it proceeds directly to the report
expectations.evidence: The poll offer was never made (assertion 1 fails), so the user never had the opportunity to decline. The decline scenario this assertion tests was never reached. The without-skill agent ended its session after the failed re-request attempt without entering any polling flow.

expectations.text: The final report does not include a poll line (user declined polling)
expectations.evidence: The final summary reads 'I attempted to re-request review from copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] but the request may not have succeeded' — no structured report and no poll line. While no poll line appears, the absence is because polling was never implemented, not because it was correctly omitted after a user decline. Marking as failed to avoid a vacuous pass.

expectations.text: The bot reviewer is re-requested via the REST /requested_reviewers endpoint (DELETE + POST), not gh pr edit
expectations.evidence: The agent attempted 'gh pr edit 91 --add-reviewer copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]' first, receiving 'Error: GraphQL: Could not resolve to a node with login of copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]'. After this failure the agent did not fall back to the REST /requested_reviewers endpoint — it noted the error and moved on. The correct DELETE + POST pattern was never attempted.

expectations.text: The commit includes a Co-authored-by trailer for the bot reviewer
expectations.evidence: The commit includes 'Co-authored-by: copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot] <copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>'. The without-skill agent correctly attributed the change to the bot in the commit trailer, as co-authorship credit is common coding-assistant behavior even without the skill.
